nodes.title: Track seen values with a Set
nodes.why: `Set` gives fast membership checks, which is perfect for duplicate detection.
nodes.alternative: Use an array and check with `includes()` each time.
nodes.tradeoff: Efficient lookups, with a little extra memory for the set.

nodes.title: Store first occurrences in a result array
nodes.why: Keeping a separate output array preserves original order of first unique values.
nodes.alternative: Convert directly to `Set` and spread at the end.
nodes.tradeoff: More explicit control, but slightly more code.

nodes.title: Add values only if they were not seen before
nodes.why: This condition ensures duplicates are skipped and only first occurrences are kept.
nodes.tradeoff: Linear-time behavior in practice, with explicit branching logic.

nodes.title: Use Set deduplication directly
nodes.why: `Set` automatically removes duplicates while keeping insertion order.
nodes.tradeoff: Very compact, but hides implementation details from learners.

nodes.title: Spread Set back into an array
nodes.why: The spread operator converts iterable Set values back into the expected array result.
nodes.tradeoff: Clean one-liner, but less step-by-step clarity.

nodes.title: Return deduplicated values in one statement
nodes.why: All work happens in one expression, so the function stays minimal.
nodes.tradeoff: Great readability once familiar, but can feel too compact for beginners.

nodes.title: Keep only first occurrence positions
nodes.why: If current index matches first index of that value, it means this is the first copy.
nodes.tradeoff: Easy conceptually, but less efficient on large arrays.

nodes.title: Use `indexOf` inside filter callback
nodes.why: `indexOf` returns the first position of each value, which lets us reject later duplicates.
nodes.tradeoff: Simple pattern, but repeated `indexOf` calls increase runtime cost.

nodes.title: Use as a readable fallback approach
nodes.why: It works well for small arrays and shows how deduplication can be expressed with array methods.
nodes.tradeoff: Readable for interviews, but typically slower for bigger inputs.
